section_path: Views of the Internet Foundation on the Digital Fairness Act
About the Internet Foundation
paragraphs.text: The Internet Foundation is an independent, public interest organisation whose mission is to promote an Internet that makes a positive contribution to both people and society. Our vision is that everyone in Sweden wants, keen and be able to use the internet. Through research and education, we contribute to understanding the impact of digitalisation on individuals and society. A central part of this work is our annual survey 'Svenskarna and the internet' (Swedenkarnaandinternet.se), which identifies Swedes' internet habits, attitudes, behaviours, opportunities and challenges.

section_path: Views of the Internet Foundation on the Digital Fairness Act
1. Summary
paragraphs.text: The Internet Foundation welcomes the European Commission’s initiative for a Digital Justice Act. The Internet Foundation recommends clarifying responsibility for design decisions and algorithmic personalisation. Furthermore, there should be transparency requirements in reporting and follow-up, and in order to increase users’ trust, understandable and easily accessible information is needed. Finally, the act should be coordinated with the DSA and DMA in order to avoid double regulation, while preserving the balance between regulation and innovation. The Internet Foundation looks forward to contributing to further work on the Digital Justice Act.

section_path: Views of the Internet Foundation on the Digital Fairness Act
2. Introduction
paragraphs.text: The Internet Foundation welcomes the European Commission’s initiative for a Digital Justice Act. The proposal addresses real and growing problems related to consumer protection online, in particular in relation to misleading design, personalisation, and marketing in digital environments. Welcomes the ambition to strengthen consumer protection, especially for children and young people who are particularly vulnerable, and to create fairer and more transparent digital markets;

section_path: Views of the Internet Foundation on the Digital Fairness Act
2. Introduction
paragraphs.text: The results of the 2024 “Fitness check on EU Consumer law on digital fairness” revealed clear shortcomings in the current level of consumer protection online. These include dark patterns, misleading marketing of influencers, addictive design of digital products and unfair commercial practices. The Digital Justice Act aims to ensure a level playing field for digital actors, strengthen consumer protection and simplify enforcement and enforcement.

section_path: Views of the Internet Foundation on the Digital Fairness Act
3. Insights from the Swedes and the Internet 2025
paragraphs.text: In the 2025 report, the Internet Foundation has specifically studied the prevalence of advertising fraud and users’ ability to detect fake advertisements on social media. The results show that six out of ten Swedes are aware that they have seen fake advertisements on social media. Only one third of those who have seen a fake advertisement have reported it, and the main reason for refraining from reporting is the perception that it does not have any effect – the advertisement still persists. This highlights a structural problem where responsibility for identifying and dealing with misleading content falls on the individual, while the causes –

section_path: Views of the Internet Foundation on the Digital Fairness Act
3. Insights from the Swedes and the Internet 2025
paragraphs.text: platforms' design, algorithms and incentives – remain unregulated.

section_path: Views of the Internet Foundation on the Digital Fairness Act
Young people and vulnerable groups
paragraphs.text: The ability to manage and value information on the internet and on social media is a challenge for all audiences. Those who want to influence and deceive are able to exploit the fast and mixed flow of information in which friends' interventions are crowded with advertising from societal actors and commercial companies – some legitimate and other clear frauds – along with opinions, influencers, established media and conspiracy theories.

section_path: Views of the Internet Foundation on the Digital Fairness Act
4. Recommendations of the Internet Foundation
paragraphs.text: Clarifies responsibility for design decisions and algorithmic personalisation. It must be clear to the user which actor is responsible for the design decision and algorithmic personalisation when using the service.

section_path: Views of the Internet Foundation on the Digital Fairness Act
4. Recommendations of the Internet Foundation
paragraphs.text: Strengthen the protection of children and young people. As an additional vulnerability, it is essential that children and young people are protected, in an appropriate and proportionate manner.

section_path: Views of the Internet Foundation on the Digital Fairness Act
4. Recommendations of the Internet Foundation
paragraphs.text: Reporting and follow-up transparency requirements; Continued transparency requirements in reporting and follow-up allow, inter alia, to identify shortcomings and areas of concern and to put in place appropriate and proportionate measures. It can also enable data-driven follow-up through cooperation with independent stakeholders.

section_path: Views of the Internet Foundation on the Digital Fairness Act
4. Recommendations of the Internet Foundation
paragraphs.text: Promote user trust through understandable information. It must be easy for the user to understand how the service is used and how, inter alia, the flow of information works, for example through clear and easily understandable terms of use.

section_path: Views of the Internet Foundation on the Digital Fairness Act
4. Recommendations of the Internet Foundation
paragraphs.text: Coordinate the act clearly with DSA and DMA. There needs to be a balance between regulation and innovation. Well-designed regulation should not have to hamper innovation and it is important to ensure that requirements do not overlap with DSA and DMA. It should be easy for service providers to understand the regulation and how they comply with it.

section_path: Views of the Internet Foundation on the Digital Fairness Act
5. Concluding remarks
paragraphs.text: The Internet Foundation looks forward to contributing to further work on the Digital Justice Act. Through our knowledge of the use of the internet by Swedes and our independent mission, we can help to highlight how legislation works in practice and how it can best strengthen people's digital security and fairness. The Internet Foundation is also working in the long term to improve the digital skills of both children and adults. Through educational measures, learning resources and initiatives that strengthen people's understanding of how the internet works, we want to contribute to making users more secure, aware and resilient in the digital environment. It takes us a bit, but the responsibility cannot lie with the individual – society's demands for those who operate on the digital arena must be clear, and these actors must take the great responsibility to protect the consumer.

section_path: Views of the Internet Foundation on the Digital Fairness Act
5. Concluding remarks
paragraphs.text: The case has been prepared by Senior Vice President Communications and Public Benefit Jannike Tillà, as well as the company lawyer Filippa Murath.
